input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technologies, serving as the physical twin for an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system. The vehicle is composed of lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements that enable ACC operation. Operated in controlled driving environments, the system is resource‑constrained and thus a suitable candidate for digital twinning to offload computation, monitor performance, and provide remote management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: 1. Data Monitoring: a user interface displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected nearby vehicle tracks, and allows operators to view, save, and generate performance reports. 2. Computation Offloading: the ACC logic (lead vehicle detection and desired speed calculation) is executed on a cloud‑based compute platform and the resulting desired speed command is sent back to the on‑board controller. 3. Remote Management: operators can enable or disable ACC, set desired speed or trigger an emergency braking command from a remote location via a low‑latency end‑to‑end communication link, supporting safety‑critical remote operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The digital twin system (DTS) integrates the physical vehicle, cloud middleware, and web‑based UI components according to community best practices for digital twin architecture. The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale vehicle equipped with advanced sensors and control modules; the middleware provides cloud‑based compute for offloading ACC processing; the UI delivers real‑time visualization and remote command interfaces. This architecture follows the Fully Autonomous Digital Twin taxonomy and supports Human‑in‑the‑Loop operations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Time synchronization among physical and digital twin components is constrained to a maximum offset of 100 ms, in line with hardware limitations, to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular standards that require tighter clock alignment. Each ROS 2 node executing within a Functional Entity (FE) exchanges timestamps using the ROS 2 Time API, ensuring that data streams from sensors and actuators are aligned to within the 100 ms budget. No explicit periodic update frequency for digital‑twin data is specified beyond this accuracy constraint.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three distinct functional entities are instantiated: a User Entity (hosting the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel nodes), a Digital Twin Entity (containing data‑management, control‑mechanism SW components such as Data Storage), and a Physical Twin Entity (wrapping device communication, pre‑processing, actuation, and automotive‑specific functions such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware platforms, creating a decentralized, distributed architecture. There is a single instance of each entity in the system; the architecture does not instantiate multiple Digital Twin replicas, nor does it use a centralized coordinator – coordination is achieved through ROS 2 message passing between the entities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin constellation follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture by mapping software components to Functional Entities and deploying each FE as a standalone ROS 2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that publish, subscribe, or provide services over ROS 2 topics. This layered organization partitions responsibilities: the User Entity handles human‑machine interaction, the Digital Twin Entity manages data life‑cycle and control logic, and the Physical Twin Entity interfaces with the vehicle’s sensors, actuators, and general automotive processing functions. Shared resources such as compute nodes, DDS‑based network, and time‑stamped sensor feeds are orchestrated via ROS 2’s canonical communication layer, ensuring consistent synchronization, data routing, and fault‑tolerance across the distributed hardware stack.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through ROS 2’s DDS‑based publish/subscribe middleware, which conforms to the RTI‑DDS or Cyclone‑DDS profiles common in automotive software stacks. The Digital Twin packages exchange messages over standard ROS 2 topic conventions (e.g., `/sensor/pointcloud`, `/actuator/command`) enabling interoperability with other ROS 2‑based Digital Twins or enterprise middleware that adheres to ROS 2/IPsec and related security standards. Time synchronization across devices leverages the ROS 2 Time API, which itself relies on the underlying system clock synchronization mechanisms mandated by ISO 23247 and automotive safety standards.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw sensor data from the vehicle’s LiDAR, motor, steering, servo actuators and vehicle state metrics are streamed to the Digital Twin over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP as the transport protocol. Within the vehicle, the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component ingests the raw LiDAR point cloud, projects it onto a 2‑D plane, and extracts object tracks and projected driving paths. All sensor streams are forwarded to a Data Translation component (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE) which serialises the data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions before publishing them on the internal ROS 2 network to downstream services such as Data Collection FE, Reporting FE and Visualisation FE. The Digital Twin then visualises the aggregated vehicle state and environment in real time on a 3‑D cockpit display. Data ingestion is triggered by periodic sensor updates; basic data‑quality checks are performed by the Data Translation and Clustering components.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate from the Digital Twin’s Control Panel User Interface FE and are routed via the internal ROS 2 network to the vehicle’s on‑board actuator interfaces (Motor Actuation FE, Steering Actuation FE, Servo Actuation FE). The Data Translation component performs unit conversion and aligns the command messages before they are transmitted over the Wi‑Fi link to the vehicle’s firmware. Commands are packaged as structured ROS 2 messages containing target positions, velocities or torque values and are validated against safety limits defined in the Control FE. No explicit authentication or encryption is described; conflict resolution relies on the firmware’s priority scheme for actuator commands. Confirmation of command execution is obtained through downstream telemetry feeds consumed by the Data Collection FE, which updates the vehicle state displayed in the 3‑D visualization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin system employs a hierarchical networking layout. Inter‑software‑component communication takes place on a ROS 2 publish/subscribe network that operates over UDP, while inter‑entity communication (between Physical Twin, Digital Twin and User interfaces) is conducted over a Wi‑Fi radio link using UDP. The topology can be considered a bus‑like architecture radiating from a Wi‑Fi access point. Bandwidth requirements are driven by the high‑rate LiDAR point‑cloud stream, though exact values are not specified; latency is expected to be low enough for real‑time visualization but quantitative figures are not provided. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms, as well as explicit security protocols (e.g., TLS, VPN, authentication), are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adheres to the ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing standard. It implements the entity‑based reference architecture defined in Part 2: (i) Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) for any physical or operational item, (ii) Data Collection and Device Control Entity for sensor data acquisition and OME control, (iii) Digital Twin Entity that digitally represents the OME, and (iv) User Entity providing interfaces for monitoring and control. Functional Entities within these entities may be implemented using OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as the technology of choice, thereby ensuring interoperability with standard industrial control protocols.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: ISO 26262-1:2018 defines the functional safety lifecycle for automotive electronic systems, requiring a systematic risk assessment, determination of the appropriate Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL), definition of safety goals, and implementation of safety mechanisms such as redundancy, fault detection, and fail-safe behaviours. ISO 15622:2018 specifies performance and test procedures for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems, including safety-critical test scenarios (e.g., detection delays, braking response times, longitudinal control accuracy) that must satisfy the ASIL levels assigned by ISO 26262. 3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0, a service requirements specification for V2X communication, prescribes cybersecurity measures for v2x services, such as authentication via digital certificates, TLS-based encryption, message integrity checks, and anti-spoofing techniques, alongside access-control policies to protect vehicle‑to‑vehicle and vehicle‑to‑infrastructure data exchanges. Together, these standards provide a framework for designing and validating safe, secure digital twin components for intelligent transport systems.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.
